channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: Xanadu Becomes First Pure-Play Photonic Quantum Computing Company to Go Public
item.description:
Curator's Take: This milestone marks a significant moment for the quantum computing industry, as Xanadu becomes the first company focused exclusively on photonic quantum computing to achieve public listing status. While IBM, Google, and other tech giants dominate headlines with their superconducting qubit systems, Xanadu's photonic approach offers unique advantages including room-temperature operation and natural compatibility with existing telecommunications infrastructure. The public listing provides Xanadu with increased capital to compete against well-funded rivals and validates photonic quantum computing as a serious contender in the race toward practical quantum advantage. This development signals growing investor confidence in alternative quantum computing architectures beyond the more established superconducting and trapped-ion platforms.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Xanadu Quantum Technologies Limited (“Xanadu” or the “Company”), a leading photonic quantum computing company, today became a publicly listed company on Nasdaq and Toronto Stock Exchange under the ticker symbol “XNDU,” following the completion of its previously announced business combination with Crane Harbor Acquisition Corp., a special purpose acquisition company. […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: China Telecom Latest Report Flags Quantum as a Key Growth Driver, But Scale Remains Uncertain
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a significant strategic shift as China Telecom, one of the world's largest telecommunications companies, formally positions quantum technology as a core growth driver rather than just an experimental venture. While the financial details remain vague, the move signals that major telecom infrastructure players are moving beyond pilot projects to serious commercial deployment of quantum communications and networking technologies. This development is particularly noteworthy given China's substantial national investments in quantum research and the growing global race to establish quantum-secure communication networks. The fact that a $70 billion revenue company is betting on quantum as a growth engine suggests the technology may be approaching commercial viability faster than many Western observers anticipated.

Insider Brief China Telecom is elevating quantum technology to a core growth driver, reporting rapid expansion in the segment even as its absolute financial contribution remains unclear and likely small relative to the company’s overall business. China Telecom reported full-year 2025 service revenues of RMB 485.4 billion, or about $70 billion US, up 0.7% year […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Wellcome Leap Q4Bio Finalists Present Quantum Approaches to Biomedical Challenges
item.description:
Curator's Take: This convergence of quantum computing with biomedical research represents a crucial inflection point where theoretical quantum advantages meet real-world health challenges. The Q4Bio program's $40 million investment signals serious institutional commitment to exploring how quantum algorithms might revolutionize drug discovery, protein folding predictions, and molecular modeling - areas where classical computers often hit computational walls. These finalist presentations offer a rare glimpse into which quantum approaches are showing the most promise for near-term biomedical applications, potentially setting the stage for the first practical quantum advantages in healthcare. The program's focus on challenge prizes rather than traditional grants suggests a results-oriented approach that could accelerate the timeline from quantum research to clinical impact.

Insider Brief Wellcome Leap’s Quantum for Bio (Q4Bio) program, a $40M research initiative with up to $10M in challenge prizes, has reached its final in-person milestone. This week, the six finalist teams gathered in Marina del Rey to present their findings to a select group of invited delegates drawn from the highest ranks of the […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Researchers from Cleveland Clinic and IBM Simulate Protein Structures with Quantum Computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research marks a significant milestone as the first demonstration of quantum computing being applied to model the electronic structure of an actual protein, moving beyond simple molecules to tackle the complex many-body quantum interactions that determine how proteins fold and function. The 303-atom Trp-cage miniprotein represents a meaningful leap in scale for quantum molecular simulation, demonstrating that today's quantum processors like IBM's Heron r2 can begin addressing real biological systems through hybrid quantum-classical workflows. While still far from modeling therapeutically relevant proteins, this work establishes a crucial proof-of-concept that quantum advantage in drug discovery and protein design may eventually be achievable as quantum hardware continues to scale. The collaboration between Cleveland Clinic and IBM also highlights how quantum computing is transitioning from purely academic research toward practical applications in healthcare and biotechnology.

Insider Brief A joint Cleveland Clinic-IBM research team has used quantum computing to demonstrate a hybrid quantum-classical workflow to approximate the electronic structure of a protein for the first time, IBM announced. The team modeled the 303-atom miniprotein Trp-cage using a quantum-centric supercomputing workflow and an IBM Quantum Heron r2. The work represents progress in […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: 15+ Global Banks Exploring Quantum Technologies [2026]
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a significant trend where major financial institutions are moving beyond theoretical interest in quantum computing to active exploration and pilot programs. The banking sector's embrace of quantum technologies is particularly noteworthy because these institutions handle massive computational workloads for risk analysis, fraud detection, and cryptographic security that could benefit enormously from quantum advantages. While the article's framing of "quantum banking" as a revolutionary system may be somewhat premature given current hardware limitations, the fact that over 15 global banks are investing resources signals growing confidence that quantum computing will eventually deliver practical benefits for financial services. This institutional momentum could accelerate quantum development timelines and help identify the most commercially viable near-term applications for the technology.

Insider Brief What is Quantum Banking? Quantum Banking is a new type of banking system that makes use of quantum technologies. This system has the potential to revolutionize the banking sector by providing more secure and efficient banking services as it can handle large amounts of data quickly and accurately. On the flip side, quantum […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Rigetti Computing Intends to Invest $100 Million in UK to Accelerate Quantum Computing Development
item.description:
Curator's Take: This investment marks a significant milestone for Rigetti as it becomes their first major expansion beyond the US market, signaling the company's confidence in the UK's growing quantum ecosystem and talent pool. The $100 million commitment positions Rigetti to tap into Britain's strong quantum research infrastructure, including partnerships with universities and access to government-backed quantum initiatives like the National Quantum Computing Centre. This move reflects the increasingly global nature of quantum computing development, where companies are seeking the best talent and research environments regardless of geography. The investment could accelerate quantum commercialization efforts by leveraging the UK's robust quantum research community while potentially creating new pathways for European quantum computing applications.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Rigetti Computing, Inc. (Nasdaq: RGTI) (“Rigetti” or the “Company”), a pioneer in full-stack quantum-classical computing, today announced that it intends to invest up to $100 million in the UK to accelerate quantum computing development, which will be the Company’s first major investment outside of the US. With this investment, the […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum computer accurately simulates real magnetic materials, reproducing national laboratory data
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a crucial validation milestone for quantum computing, demonstrating that current quantum hardware can accurately reproduce real-world materials data from national laboratories rather than just theoretical toy problems. The ability to simulate actual magnetic materials with quantum computers addresses one of the technology's most promising near-term applications, as classical computers fundamentally struggle with the exponential complexity of quantum many-body systems found in advanced materials. This work bridges the gap between quantum computing's theoretical potential and practical materials science, potentially accelerating the discovery of new superconductors, magnetic storage devices, and quantum materials that could revolutionize electronics and energy storage.

Studying and designing novel materials is a central application of quantum mechanics. Chemists, materials scientists, and physicists focus on subtle interactions in quantum materials and to uncover them they rely on sophisticated computational and experimental techniques. Computer simulations that connect microscopic quantum interactions to measurable material properties complement experimental data to connect structure to function—but classical computers can struggle to simulate those properties. Fortunately, scientists today have a new tool in their toolbox: quantum computers.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Unlocking scalable entanglement will enable next-generation quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights one of the most fundamental challenges in scaling quantum computers beyond today's prototype systems. While current quantum devices can create entanglement between small numbers of qubits, achieving stable, controllable entanglement across hundreds or thousands of qubits remains the holy grail that will unlock quantum computing's transformative potential. The research appears to address critical technical hurdles around maintaining quantum coherence and reducing errors as systems grow larger, which is essential for running the complex algorithms that could revolutionize drug discovery, materials science, and cryptography. Successfully scaling entanglement represents a crucial bridge between today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and the fault-tolerant quantum computers needed for practical quantum advantage.

Quantum computing promises to transform our world in rapid, radical and revolutionary ways: solving in seconds problems that would take classical computers years, accelerating the discovery of new medicines, creating sustainable materials, optimizing complex systems, and strengthening cybersecurity. It does so using qubits, the quantum counterparts of classical bits, which can occupy multiple states simultaneously and enable a fundamentally new kind of computation.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Robust continuous-variable multipartite entanglement in circular arrays of nonlinear waveguides
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a significant advance in creating robust, scalable entangled states using continuous-variable quantum systems, which could be crucial for practical quantum computing and communication networks. The researchers have developed a clever approach using circular arrays of nonlinear waveguides that can maintain strong multipartite entanglement regardless of system size or physical variations - a major engineering challenge that has limited previous implementations. What makes this particularly exciting is the inherent robustness of their phase-matched design, which means the entanglement persists even when experimental conditions aren't perfectly controlled, addressing one of the key obstacles to scaling up quantum systems. This work could pave the way for more practical continuous-variable quantum processors that can handle real-world imperfections while maintaining the quantum advantages needed for computation.

Encoding continuous-variable quantum information in the optical domain has recently enabled the generation of large entangled states, yet robust implementation remains a challenge. Here, we present a straightforward protocol for generating multipartite entanglement based on spontaneous parametric down-conversion in a circular array of quadratic nonlinear waveguides. We provide a rigorous theoretical framework, including comprehensive derivations of the propagation equations and the identification of regimes where analytical solutions are possible. Crucially, our approach identifies the pump and detection configurations required to sustain and measure multipartite full inseparability across arbitrary propagation distances and for any number of waveguides $N=4 n$. This regime, elusive to standard numerical methods, represents a key requirement for scalable quantum protocols. Our scheme is inherently robust as it relies on phase-matched propagation eigenmodes, making it resilient against variations in sample length, coupling, and nonlinearity.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: A unified quantum computing quantum Monte Carlo framework through structured state preparation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a significant advancement in quantum computing by transforming QCQMC from a ground-state-only tool into a versatile framework that can tackle excited states, optimization problems, and even finite-temperature calculations. The key innovation lies in replacing the standard VQE approach with specialized quantum circuits tailored to each computational task, while consistently using quantum Monte Carlo to enhance accuracy across diverse applications from molecular chemistry to graph optimization. What makes this particularly compelling is the demonstration that shallow quantum circuits combined with classical pre-training can achieve near-exact results for weakly correlated systems, potentially making quantum advantage more accessible on near-term devices. The framework's ability to handle such a broad range of quantum simulation challenges through a unified approach could streamline the development of practical quantum algorithms across multiple scientific domains.

We extend Quantum Computing Quantum Monte Carlo (QCQMC) beyond ground-state energy estimation by systematically constructing the quantum circuits used for state preparation. Replacing the original Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) prescription with task-adapted unitaries, we show that QCQMC can address excited-state spectra via Variational Fast Forwarding and the Variational Unitary Matrix Product Operator (VUMPO), combinatorial optimization via a symmetry-preserving VQE ansatz, and finite-temperature observables via Haar-random unitaries. Benchmarks on molecular, condensed-matter, nuclear-structure, and graph-optimization problems demostrate that the QMC diffusion step consistently improves the energy accuracy of the underlying state-preparation method across all tested domains. For weakly correlated systems, VUMPO achieves near-exact energies with significantly shallower circuits by offloading optimization to a classical tensor-network pre-training step, while for strongly correlated systems, the QMC correction becomes essential. We further provide a proof-of-concept demonstration that Haar-random basis state preparation within QCQMC yields finite-temperature estimates from pure-state dynamics.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Adaptive Negativity Estimation via Collective Measurements
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of quantum computing's fundamental challenges: efficiently measuring how entangled quantum states actually are, which is crucial for verifying quantum advantage and debugging quantum algorithms. The clever combination of collective measurements with LSTM neural networks represents a significant step forward in quantum state characterization, allowing researchers to adaptively optimize their measurement strategy in real-time rather than using fixed protocols. What makes this particularly exciting is the demonstrated improvement over non-adaptive approaches, suggesting that machine learning can genuinely enhance our ability to probe quantum systems with limited experimental resources. This adaptive framework could prove invaluable for quantum error correction and state preparation verification in near-term quantum devices where measurement overhead is a critical bottleneck.

This paper explores an efficient method for entanglement quantification in two-qubit and qubit-qutrit quantum systems based upon the framework of collective measurements in conjunction with machine learning. We introduce an adaptive measurement procedure in which measurement settings are dynamically adjusted based on prior measurement outcomes aiming to optimize the inference precision given a limited number of these measurement settings. The procedure makes use of the Long Short-Term Memory networks to recurrently process collective measurements on two copies of the investigated states. Obtained results demonstrate the tangible benefits of the adaptive measurements in comparison to previously described non-adaptive strategies.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Kardashev scale Quantum Computing for Bitcoin Mining
item.description:
Curator's Take: This fascinating paper tackles a widely misunderstood aspect of Bitcoin's quantum vulnerability by rigorously analyzing the actual cost of quantum mining, not just the theoretical speedup from Grover's algorithm. While quantum computers could theoretically provide a quadratic advantage in Bitcoin mining, the researchers demonstrate that implementing this attack would require absolutely staggering resources - up to 10^23 qubits and power consumption approaching that of entire stellar civilizations for current Bitcoin difficulty levels. The study provides crucial context to public discussions about Bitcoin's quantum security by showing that even with a working quantum computer, the mining threat is fundamentally different from and far less practical than the well-known signature attacks via Shor's algorithm. This work exemplifies how moving from theoretical quantum advantage to practical implementation often reveals prohibitive overhead costs that make seemingly promising quantum attacks economically infeasible.

Bitcoin already faces a quantum threat through Shor attacks on elliptic-curve signatures. This paper isolates the other component that public discussion often conflates with it: mining. Grover's algorithm halves the exponent of brute-force search, promising a quadratic edge to any quantum miner of Bitcoin. Exactly how large that edge grows depends on fault-tolerant hardware. No prior study has costed that hardware end to end. We build an open-source estimator that sweeps the full attack surface: reversible oracles for double-SHA-256 mining and RIPEMD-based address preimages, surface-code factory sizing, fleet logistics under Nakamoto-consensus timing, and Kardashev-scale energy accounting. A parametric sweep over difficulty bits b, runtime caps, and target success probabilities reveals a sharp transition. At the most favourable partial-preimage setting (b = 32, 2^224 marked states), a superconducting surface-code fleet still requires about 10^8 physical qubits and about 10^4 MW. That load is comparable to a large national grid. Tightening to Bitcoin's January 2025 mainnet difficulty (b about 79) explodes the bill to about 10^23 qubits and about 10^25 W, approaching the Kardashev Type II threshold. These numbers settle a narrower question than "Is Bitcoin quantum-secure?" Once Grover mining is lifted from asymptotic query counts to fault-tolerant physical cost, practical quantum mining collapses under oracle, distillation, and fleet overhead. To push mining into non-trivial consensus effects, one must invoke astronomical quantum fleets operating at energy scales that lie far above present-day civilization.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Send the Key in Cleartext: Halving Key Consumption while Preserving Unconditional Security in QKD Authentication
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a fundamental efficiency bottleneck in quantum key distribution that has plagued the field since its inception. The authentication overhead in QKD systems typically consumes a significant portion of the very quantum keys they're designed to protect, creating a frustrating catch-22 where secure communication eats into its own security budget. By cleverly redesigning the authentication protocol to use a single key instead of two per communication round, this work could dramatically improve the practical viability of QKD networks, effectively doubling their net key generation rates. The breakthrough lies in maintaining the mathematical rigor of information-theoretic security while halving the cryptographic overhead, potentially making quantum-secured communications far more economically attractive for real-world deployment.

Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) protocols require Information-Theoretically Secure (ITS) authentication of the classical channel to preserve the unconditional security of the distilled key. Standard ITS schemes are based on one-time keys: once a key is used to authenticate a message, it must be discarded. Since QKD requires mutual authentication, two independent one-time keys are typically consumed per round, imposing a non-trivial overhead on the net secure key rate. In this work, we present the authentication-with-response scheme, a novel ITS authentication scheme based on $\varepsilon$-Almost Strongly Universal$_2$ ($\varepsilon$-ASU$_2$) functions, whose IT security can be established in the Universal Composability (UC) framework. The scheme achieves mutual authentication consuming a single one-time key per QKD round, halving key consumption compared to the state-of-the-art.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Maximizing Qubit Throughput under Buffer Decoherence and Variability in Generation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a critical bottleneck in quantum networks that bridges theoretical quantum communication with real-world implementation challenges. The work addresses the fundamental tension between wanting qubits ready when needed versus the inevitable degradation that occurs when qubits sit waiting in buffers, a problem that becomes especially acute as quantum networks scale beyond laboratory demonstrations. By framing this as an admission control problem with adaptive learning, the authors provide practical tools for optimizing quantum communication systems like quantum key distribution networks, where maintaining high qubit fidelity is essential for security. The cross-application to IoT sensing systems also highlights how quantum networking research is developing solutions that could benefit broader technological infrastructure.

Quantum communication networks require transmission of high-fidelity, uncoded qubits for applications such as entanglement distribution and quantum key distribution. However, current implementations are constrained by limited buffer capacity and qubit decoherence, which degrades qubit quality while waiting in the buffer. A key challenge arises from the stochastic nature of qubit generation, there exists a random delay (D) between the initiation of a generation request and the availability of the qubit. This induces a fundamental trade off early initiation increases buffer waiting time and hence decoherence, whereas delayed initiation leads to server idling and reduced throughput. We model this system as an admission control problem in a finite buffer queue, where the reward associated with each job is a decreasing function of its sojourn time. We derive analytical conditions under which a simple "no lag" policy where a new qubit is generated immediately upon the availability of buffer space is optimal. To address scenarios with unknown system parameters, we further develop a Bayesian learning framework that adaptively optimizes the admission policy. In addition to quantum communication systems, the proposed model is applicable to delay sensitive IoT sensing and service systems.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Mass-correction-induced enhancement of quantum correlations even beyond entanglement in the $e^{+}e^{-} \rightarrow J/ψ\rightarrow Λ(pπ^{-}) \barΛ(\bar{p}π^{+})$ process at the BESIII experiment under memory effects
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article explores an intriguing intersection between high-energy particle physics and quantum information theory by analyzing quantum correlations in baryon-antibaryon pairs produced at the BESIII collider experiment. The researchers demonstrate that including realistic mass corrections actually strengthens quantum non-locality and Bell inequality violations in these particle systems, revealing how fundamental physics parameters can enhance rather than diminish quantum effects. What makes this particularly fascinating is the discovery that classical correlations can help preserve quantum coherence against decoherence, suggesting that particle collision experiments could serve as natural laboratories for studying quantum information phenomena. While highly theoretical, this work opens new avenues for understanding how quantum correlations manifest in high-energy physics and potentially points toward novel quantum sensing applications using particle accelerators.

In this work, we derive the bipartite density matrix for the $e^{+}e^{-} \rightarrow J/ψ\rightarrow Λ(pπ^{-}) \barΛ(\bar{p}π^{+})$ process at BESIII. We evaluate the impact of mass corrections and memory effects (within Markovian and non-Markovian regimes) on quantum correlations even beyond entanglement. The dependence of these quantum properties on the scattering angle $\varphi$ is analyzed, with a particular focus on the impact of mass corrections. By comparing massless and mass-corrected scenarios, we demonstrate that the inclusion of mass effects enhances the maximum violation of the Bell inequality. While the qualitative temporal behavior remains unchanged, mass corrections quantitatively modify the angular distribution and introduce additional extrema at $\varphi=0$ and $\varphi=π$, thereby strengthening non-local correlations without altering their fundamental dynamical origin. An examination of the hierarchy of quantum correlations in baryon-antibaryon systems yields partial confirmation: $\text{Bell Nonlocality} \subset \text{Steering} \subset \text{Entanglement} \subset \text{Discord}$. Additionally, our results show that classical correlations serve to mitigate the decoherence and the decay of quantum correlations. This interplay between classical and quantum correlations suggests practical applications in quantum information and provides a robust framework for investigating baryon-antibaryon interactions.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: A counterexample to the strong spin alignment conjecture
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article delivers a significant blow to a fundamental conjecture in quantum information theory by providing the first concrete counterexample to the strong spin alignment conjecture, which has important implications for understanding quantum channel capacities and information transmission. The counterexample cleverly exploits the subtle quantum mechanical principle that certain two-qubit states cannot be embedded consistently within a larger three-qubit system, revealing deep constraints on how quantum correlations can be globally realized. While this result might seem purely theoretical, it actually impacts our understanding of quantum error correction and channel coding limits, areas crucial for building reliable quantum computers. The work also opens new research directions by proposing a refined "compatibility-constrained" version of the conjecture that could better capture the true behavior of these quantum systems.

The spin alignment conjecture was originally formulated in connection with the additivity of coherent information for a class of quantum channels known as platypus channels. Recently, a stronger majorization-based version was proposed by M. A. Alhejji and E. Knill [Commun. Math. Phys. 405, 119, 2024], asserting that the spectrum of the alignment operator is always majorized by that of the perfectly aligned configuration. In this letter, we show that this strong spin alignment conjecture is false in general by constructing an explicit counterexample in the smallest unresolved case, namely three qubits. The example uses two-body states that are not jointly compatible with any single three-qubit global state, which naturally leads to a compatibility-constrained variant of the conjecture.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: John Martinis, Nobel Prize–Winning Physicist, Joins White House Science And Technology Panel
item.description:
Curator's Take: This appointment represents a significant boost for quantum computing's influence in federal policy, as Martinis brings deep technical expertise from his pioneering work on superconducting qubits and his leadership role in achieving Google's quantum supremacy milestone. Having a Nobel laureate with hands-on quantum hardware experience advising the White House could accelerate national quantum initiatives and help ensure America's quantum policies are grounded in cutting-edge scientific understanding. The timing is particularly crucial as the U.S. faces intensifying global competition in quantum technologies, where strategic government support and coordination between academia, industry, and federal agencies will be essential for maintaining technological leadership. This article signals that quantum computing continues to rise in national security and economic priority discussions at the highest levels of government.

Insider Brief President Donald J. Trump appointed Nobel laureate John Martinis and a group of technology leaders to the President’s Council of Advisors on Science and Technology to guide U.S. science and innovation policy. Martinis, known for his work in superconducting quantum computing and prior leadership at Google Quantum AI, brings quantum expertise to a […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: OQD Launches Open-Source Collaboration with WD and QuScript to Advance Quantum Error Correction
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration marks a significant step toward making quantum error correction more accessible through open-source development, with Western Digital's expertise in data reliability bringing valuable perspective to one of quantum computing's most critical challenges. The partnership is particularly noteworthy because it bridges the gap between classical data storage engineering and quantum error correction, potentially accelerating solutions by leveraging WD's decades of experience in maintaining data integrity at scale. Open-source approaches to quantum error correction could democratize access to these essential tools and foster broader community innovation, which is crucial as the field moves toward fault-tolerant quantum systems. The involvement of a major storage technology company also signals growing industry recognition that quantum error correction will be fundamental to practical quantum computing applications.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Open Quantum Design (OQD) has announced a new open-source Error Correction Working Group with QuScript and WD (Western Digital, Nasdaq: WDC), the storage foundation of the AI-driven data economy and a leader in precision engineering and large-scale data reliability, to advance quantum error correction (QEC) — an essential step toward fault-tolerant quantum computing. The collaboration brings […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Finite Temperature Lanczos Method
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article introduces a clever quantum algorithm that tackles one of the most computationally demanding problems in quantum physics: calculating how quantum systems behave at finite temperatures, which is crucial for understanding real-world materials and quantum devices. The Quantum Finite Temperature Lanczos Method represents a significant advance because it sidesteps the exponential scaling that makes classical computers struggle with these calculations, potentially opening up new frontiers in quantum materials research and condensed matter physics. What makes this work particularly noteworthy is its practical focus on noise robustness and systematic analysis of trade-offs between computational resources and accuracy, suggesting it could actually be implemented on near-term quantum computers rather than remaining purely theoretical. The successful demonstration on the transverse-field Ising model, a workhorse system for testing quantum algorithms, provides strong evidence that this approach could scale to more complex quantum many-body problems that are currently beyond our computational reach.

The computation of thermal properties of quantum many-body systems is a central challenge in our understanding of quantum mechanics. We introduce the Quantum Finite Temperature Lanczos Method (QFTLM), which extends the finite-temperature Lanczos method to quantum computers by combining real-time quantum Krylov methods with efficient preparation of typical states for trace estimation. This approach enables the computation of thermal expectation values while avoiding the exponential scaling inherent to classical exact simulation techniques. Numerical experiments on the transverse-field Ising model show that QFTLM can reproduce thermal observables over a wide temperature range. We further analyze the influence of Krylov dimension, number of trace-estimator states, and Trotter error, and show that suitable regularization is essential for robustness in noisy settings. These results establish QFTLM as a promising framework for finite-temperature quantum simulation.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Dual-rail superconducting qubits generate high-fidelity logical entanglement, study finds
item.description:
Curator's Take: This study represents a significant step toward practical quantum error correction, as dual-rail encoding offers a promising path to creating more stable logical qubits from noisy physical ones. By achieving high-fidelity logical entanglement between these encoded qubits, researchers are demonstrating that we can maintain quantum coherence even while adding the redundancy needed for error correction. This approach is particularly exciting for superconducting quantum systems because it could help bridge the gap between today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and the fault-tolerant quantum computers needed for breakthrough applications. The ability to generate reliable logical entanglement is a crucial building block for scaling up quantum computers while keeping error rates manageable.

Quantum computers, systems that process information leveraging quantum mechanical effects, could outperform classical computers on some advanced tasks. These systems rely on qubits, the fundamental units of quantum information, that become linked via an effect known as quantum entanglement and share a unified quantum state.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: IBM, Academic Partners Use Quantum Computer to Reproduce Key Material Properties, Testing Early Scientific Usefulness
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration represents a significant milestone in demonstrating that current noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices can tackle real scientific problems, not just theoretical demonstrations. By successfully reproducing experimentally measured magnetic material properties, the team has shown that quantum computers may contribute to materials science research even before we achieve full quantum error correction - a timeline that could still be years away. This finding is particularly important because it suggests the quantum advantage for certain scientific applications might arrive sooner than expected, potentially accelerating discoveries in areas like drug development, catalyst design, and energy storage materials. The work validates the growing consensus that NISQ-era quantum computers, despite their limitations, can provide genuine scientific value when applied to carefully selected problems that align with their current capabilities.

Insider Brief A collaboration between IBM and academic researchers indicates that a quantum computer can reproduce experimentally measured properties of a real magnetic material, offering early evidence that today’s machines may contribute to practical scientific problems before full error correction is achieved. In a new preprint on arXiv, researchers from Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Purdue […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: The 27-qubit Counterexample to the LU-LC Conjecture is Minimal
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article resolves a fundamental question in quantum graph state theory by proving that 27 qubits is indeed the smallest system where local unitary equivalence and local Clifford equivalence diverge - a distinction that has profound implications for understanding quantum state transformations and error correction. The result settles nearly two decades of uncertainty since the original counterexample was discovered in 2007, providing crucial theoretical grounding for quantum algorithm designers who need to understand which graph state transformations are possible using different types of operations. By connecting this problem to classical coding theory through triorthogonal and Reed-Muller codes, the work demonstrates how quantum information theory continues to bridge with established mathematical frameworks. This precision in understanding equivalence classes becomes increasingly important as quantum computers scale toward the fault-tolerant regime where efficient state preparation and manipulation are critical.

It was once conjectured that two graph states are local unitary (LU) equivalent if and only if they are local Clifford (LC) equivalent. This so-called LU-LC conjecture was disproved in 2007, as a pair of 27-qubit graph states that are LU-equivalent, but not LC-equivalent, was discovered. We prove that this counterexample to the LU-LC conjecture is minimal. In other words, for graph states on up to 26 qubits, the notions of LU-equivalence and LC-equivalence coincide. This result is obtained by studying the structure of 2-local complementation, a special case of the recently introduced r-local complementation, and a generalization of the well-known local complementation. We make use of a connection with triorthogonal codes and Reed-Muller codes.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Cascaded Metasurface Interferometer for Multipath Interference with Classical and Quantum Light
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a significant breakthrough in addressing one of quantum photonics' most pressing scalability challenges: the exponential growth in optical components needed as quantum systems expand. Traditional beamsplitters require a quadratic increase in components for fully connected networks, but these metasurface-based multiport beamsplitters can manipulate multiple light paths simultaneously at the wavelength scale, offering a much more compact and scalable solution. The researchers successfully demonstrated the technology works for both classical light and quantum states, showing controllable interference patterns and photon correlations across multiple paths. This represents a crucial step toward practical large-scale quantum photonic processors and could enable more sophisticated quantum computing architectures that were previously impractical due to size and complexity constraints.

Beamsplitters represent fundamental components in both classical and quantum optical systems, enabling the distribution of light, as well as the generation of interference, superposition and entanglement. However, optical networks constructed from conventional bulk 2x2-beamsplitters encounter inherent scalability issues, as the number of required beamsplitters scales quadratically with the number of optical modes for a fully connected network. Metasurfaces offer a promising route to overcome these constraints. By manipulating light at the wavelength scale compact optical components with advanced functionalities can be constructed, which address several modes simultaneously. In this work, we design and experimentally utilize a metasurface as a multiport beamsplitter. Furthermore, we realize a multimode interferometer composed of two cascaded metasurfaces. We characterize the individual and cascaded metasurfaces using classical light, showing controllable splitting ratios through tunable phase relations. We then expand the approach to quantum light, employing single photons to demonstrate second- and third-order photon correlations, as well as single photon interference across multiple spatial paths. These results establish metasurface-based multiport beamsplitters as a scalable and reconfigurable platform bridging classical and quantum photonics.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Uncertainty Quantification for Quantum Computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This comprehensive review tackles one of quantum computing's most pressing challenges by bridging the gap between mathematical rigor and practical quantum error analysis. The work is particularly timely as the field grapples with noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices where understanding and quantifying uncertainty becomes crucial for meaningful computational results. By translating quantum noise and errors into the well-established language of uncertainty quantification, this approach offers mathematicians and computational scientists a familiar framework for addressing reliability challenges that have long plagued quantum algorithms. The emphasis on mathematically grounded error mitigation and validation methods could prove essential as quantum computing moves toward practical applications where statistical confidence in results becomes as important as the quantum advantage itself.

This review is designed to introduce mathematicians and computational scientists to quantum computing (QC) through the lens of uncertainty quantification (UQ) by presenting a mathematically rigorous and accessible narrative for understanding how noise and intrinsic randomness shape quantum computational outcomes in the language of mathematics. By grounding quantum computation in statistical inference, we highlight how mathematical tools such as probabilistic modeling, stochastic analysis, Bayesian inference, and sensitivity analysis, can directly address error propagation and reliability challenges in today's quantum devices. We also connect these methods to key scientific priorities in the field, including scalable uncertainty-aware algorithms and characterization of correlated errors. The purpose is to narrow the conceptual divide between applied mathematics, scientific computing and quantum information sciences, demonstrating how mathematically rooted UQ methodologies can guide validation, error mitigation, and principled algorithm design for emerging quantum technologies, in order to address challenges and opportunities present in modern-day quantum high performance and fault-tolerant quantum computing paradigms.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Explicit States with Two-sided Long-Range Magic
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a fundamental question in quantum computing: what makes certain quantum states inherently more powerful than others for achieving quantum advantage. The authors have identified explicit quantum states with "two-sided long-range magic" - a property that makes them provably impossible to create using simple quantum circuits, placing them in a higher tier of computational complexity. This work is particularly significant because it connects abstract theoretical concepts about quantum resources to concrete examples like topological quantum states, which are promising candidates for fault-tolerant quantum computing. By establishing rigorous boundaries between different levels of quantum computational power, this research helps map the landscape of what's possible with near-term quantum devices versus what requires the full power of fault-tolerant quantum computers.

Nonstabilizerness, or magic, is a necessary resource for quantum advantage beyond the classically simulatable Clifford framework. Recent works have begun to chart the structure of magic in many-body states, introducing the concepts of long-range magic -- nonstabilizerness that cannot be removed by finite-depth local unitary (FDU) circuits -- and the magic hierarchy, which classifies quantum circuits by alternating layers of Clifford and FDUs. In this work, we construct explicit states that provably possess two-sided long-range magic, a stronger form of magic meaning that they cannot be prepared by a Clifford circuit and a FDU in either order, thus placing them provably outside the first level of the magic hierarchy. Our examples include the ``magical cat" state, $|ψ\rangle \propto |0^n\rangle + |+^n\rangle$, and ground states of certain nonabelian topological orders. These results provide new examples and proof techniques for circuit complexity, and in doing so, reveal the connection between long-range magic, the structure of many-body phases, and the principles of quantum error correction.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Inspired Vehicular Network Optimization for Intelligent Decision Making in Smart Cities
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research demonstrates how quantum-inspired optimization techniques can tackle real-world smart city challenges without requiring actual quantum hardware, making advanced algorithms accessible for immediate deployment. The QIVNOM framework's ability to jointly optimize vehicle communication networks and traffic control represents a significant step toward more resilient urban transportation systems, showing particularly impressive performance improvements during network outages and infrastructure failures. While the quantum connection is primarily algorithmic rather than hardware-based, the work illustrates how concepts from quantum computing can inspire classical solutions to complex multi-objective optimization problems that plague modern smart cities. The substantial latency reductions of 20-22% during both normal and incident conditions could translate to meaningful safety and efficiency improvements for autonomous vehicle networks.

Connected and automated vehicles require city-scale coordination under strict latency and reliability constraints. However, many existing approaches optimize communication and mobility separately, which can degrade performance during network outages and under compute contention. This paper presents QIVNOM, a quantum-inspired framework that jointly optimizes vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) and vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2I) communication together with urban traffic control on classical edge--cloud hardware, without requiring a quantum processor. QIVNOM encodes candidate routing--signal plans as probabilistic superpositions and updates them using sphere-projected gradients with annealed sampling to minimize a regularized objective. An entanglement-style regularizer couples networking and mobility decisions, while Tchebycheff multi-objective scalarization with feasibility projection enforces constraints on latency and reliability. The proposed framework is evaluated in METR-LA--calibrated SUMO--OMNeT++/Veins simulations over a $5\times5$~km urban map with IEEE 802.11p and 5G NR sidelink. Results show that QIVNOM reduces mean end-to-end latency to 57.3~ms, approximately $20\%$ lower than the best baseline. Under incident conditions, latency decreases from 79~ms to 62~ms ($-21.5\%$), while under roadside unit (RSU) outages, it decreases from 86~ms to 67~ms ($-22.1\%$). Packet delivery reaches $96.7\%$ (an improvement of $+2.3$ percentage points), and reliability remains $96.7\%$ overall, including $96.8\%$ under RSU outages versus $94.1\%$ for the baseline. In corridor-closure scenarios, travel performance also improves, with average travel time reduced to 12.8~min and congestion lowered to $33\%$, compared with 14.5~min and $37\%$ for the baseline.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Quantum AI Adopts Dual-Modality Strategy with Neutral Atom Expansion
item.description:
Curator's Take: Google's decision to pursue both superconducting and neutral atom quantum computing platforms represents a significant strategic pivot that acknowledges the fundamental uncertainties still facing the field. This dual-modality approach hedges against the possibility that superconducting qubits alone may not achieve the scale and coherence needed for practical quantum advantage, while neutral atoms offer compelling advantages in connectivity and potential for larger qubit counts. The move follows similar multi-platform strategies from other major players like Amazon and IBM, suggesting the industry is maturing beyond single-technology bets toward more diversified research portfolios. This expansion could accelerate progress by allowing Google to leverage the unique strengths of each platform while cross-pollinating insights between their hardware teams.

Google Quantum AI has announced a major strategic expansion of its research roadmap, adding neutral atom quantum computing to its established superconducting hardware program. Led by founder Hartmut Neven, the move signals a shift toward a multi-platform approach to reach commercially relevant quantum advantage. While Google remains confident in the timeline for superconducting systems by [...] The post Google Quantum AI Adopts Dual-Modality Strategy with Neutral Atom Expansion appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: New Zealand Researchers Report Advance in Optical Ising Machine for Hybrid Quantum Optimization
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research highlights an important trend in quantum computing where hybrid classical-quantum systems are emerging as practical stepping stones toward full-scale quantum advantage. Optical Ising machines represent a particularly promising approach because they can leverage quantum-inspired physics to tackle complex optimization problems without requiring the extreme engineering challenges of fault-tolerant quantum computers. The New Zealand team's focus on scalability is especially significant, as many current quantum and quantum-inspired systems struggle to maintain performance advantages as problem sizes grow. While we await the arrival of fully fault-tolerant quantum computers, these hybrid optimization devices could deliver real-world value in logistics, finance, and materials science much sooner than traditional quantum computing timelines suggest.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — With the world’s first scalable, fault-tolerant quantum computer expected to be years away, a group of New Zealand researchers has developed an exciting hybrid device that could solve big, intractable problems sooner. Research Fellow, Dr Liam Quinn of Te Whai Ao — Dodd-Walls Centre is leading the work, considered of […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Washington Begins to Treat Quantum Technology as a Strategic Priority
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals a crucial shift as quantum technology moves from research curiosity to national security imperative in Washington's policy circles. The bipartisan engagement suggests lawmakers are beginning to understand quantum's dual nature as both an enormous economic opportunity and a potential threat to current cryptographic systems that underpin everything from financial transactions to government communications. What makes this particularly significant is the timing - as quantum computers edge closer to practical applications, the window for establishing regulatory frameworks, funding priorities, and international cooperation agreements is narrowing rapidly. The fact that these discussions are happening now, rather than after quantum advantage becomes widespread, suggests policymakers may finally be getting ahead of the technology curve for once.

Insider Brief Tuesday in Washington offered a clear picture of where quantum technology now stands, not as a future concept, but as a present responsibility. Meetings on Capitol Hill, organized by Optica, brought together lawmakers, federal agencies, and leaders from across the quantum ecosystem to focus on what comes next. The conversations were not about […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Paves a Two-Lane Quantum Roadmap by Adding Neutral Atom Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals a major strategic shift for Google Quantum AI, which has built its quantum supremacy claims and current leadership position around superconducting qubits but is now hedging its bets by exploring neutral atom systems. The move reflects growing industry recognition that different quantum hardware approaches may excel at different types of problems, with neutral atoms potentially offering advantages in connectivity and scalability that could complement Google's superconducting expertise. By diversifying its quantum hardware portfolio, Google is positioning itself to capitalize on whichever technology proves most viable for near-term commercial applications, while also acknowledging that the path to fault-tolerant quantum computing may require multiple parallel approaches rather than betting everything on a single platform.

Insider Brief Google Quantum AI, which was a pioneer in the superconducting approach to quantum computing, announced that it is expanding beyond that core approach to add neutral atom quantum computing, a move the company says will accelerate progress toward commercially useful machines. The plan, discussed in a recent Google Quantum AI blog post, would […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Scalable quantum circuit generation for iterative ground state approximation using Majorana Propagation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work tackles one of quantum computing's most promising near-term applications - finding ground states of molecular systems - by cleverly combining classical preprocessing with quantum circuit design. The researchers demonstrate their ADAPT-VMPE algorithm can generate practical quantum circuits for systems as large as 100 qubits while maintaining polynomial scaling, which is crucial for making quantum chemistry simulations feasible on near-term devices. What makes this particularly exciting is their focus on a real-world photosensitizer molecule used in cancer treatment, showing how quantum algorithms could eventually accelerate drug discovery and materials science. The theoretical guarantees on approximation error and the polynomial complexity bounds suggest this approach could bridge the gap between today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and useful quantum advantage in molecular simulation.

We introduce the Adaptive Derivative-Assembled Pseudo-Trotter ansatz Variational Majorana Propagation Eigensolver (ADAPT-VMPE), a quantum-inspired classical algorithm that exploits Majorana Propagation (MP) to produce circuits for approximating the ground state of molecular Hamiltonians. Equipped with the theoretical guarantees of MP, which provide controllable bounds on the approximation error, ADAPT-VMPE offers an efficient and scalable approach for iterative ansatz construction. A theoretical analysis of the computational complexity demonstrates that it is polynomial in both the number of qubits and the number of iterations. We present an in-depth analysis of circuit construction strategies, analyzing their impact on convergence and provide practical guidance for efficient ansatz generation. Using ADAPT-VMPE, we construct up to 100-qubit ansätze for a strongly correlated photosensitizer currently undergoing human clinical trials for cancer treatment. Our results demonstrate that constant overlap with the ground state across system sizes can be reached in polynomial time with polynomially sized circuits.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum simulation of Motzkin spin chain with Rydberg atoms
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a breakthrough pathway for experimentally realizing Motzkin spin chains, exotic quantum systems that have tantalized theorists for years due to their unusual entanglement properties that violate fundamental scaling laws. Unlike typical quantum systems where entanglement grows with the boundary area between regions, Motzkin chains exhibit logarithmic scaling that makes them notoriously difficult to simulate on classical computers, yet they hold deep connections to topological phases and even string theory through AdS/CFT correspondence. The researchers' proposed Rydberg atom platform offers a realistic experimental route to create these mathematical constructs in the lab, potentially opening doors to studying a whole new class of highly entangled quantum phases that could inform both fundamental physics and future quantum technologies. This work exemplifies how programmable quantum simulators are becoming powerful tools for exploring exotic quantum matter that exists at the edge of what's computationally tractable.

Motzkin spin chain is a well-known mathematical model with connections to symmetry-protected topological phases, such as the Haldane phase, as well as to concepts in the AdS/CFT correspondence. They exhibit highly entangled ground states that violate the area law and are exceptionally difficult to simulate with conventional numerical methods. Numerical simulations of the Motzkin ground state become further challenging at large system sizes due to their high-dimensional spin structure, rendering it a natural test bed for quantum simulation with ultra-cold systems. Here, we propose a Rydberg-atom based quantum simulation scheme that effectively realizes Motzkin spins using an experimentally accessible set of parameters. We show that the resulting effective Motzkin ground state reproduces the characteristic entanglement scaling and the block-structure properties of the reduced density matrix associated with the ideal Motzkin state. Our results establish a pathway toward a concrete experimental realization of Motzkin spins beyond purely mathematical constructions, opening avenues for exploring other similar exotic non-area-law entangled phases in programmable Rydberg simulators.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: NRFC Investment Accelerates SQC’s Atomic-Scale Manufacturing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This investment marks a significant milestone for Silicon Quantum Computing's unique atomic-scale manufacturing approach, which positions them as the sole company worldwide capable of fabricating quantum chips with atomic precision. The AUD$20 million from Australia's National Reconstruction Fund Corporation not only validates SQC's silicon-based quantum technology but also strengthens Australia's position in the global quantum race, particularly in the critical hardware manufacturing sector where precision at the atomic level remains one of the field's greatest technical challenges. What makes this particularly noteworthy is SQC's claim of already delivering quantum-enhanced AI products to enterprise and government clients today, suggesting their technology has moved beyond pure research into practical applications. This combination of manufacturing precision and commercial readiness could accelerate the timeline for practical quantum computing deployment, especially in applications where silicon-based quantum systems offer advantages over other qubit technologies.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — The National Reconstruction Fund Corporation (NRFC) has invested AUD$20 million in Silicon Quantum Computing (SQC), a global leader in the field of quantum computing. SQC is the only company in the world capable of manufacturing quantum chips with atomic precision while delivering quantum enhanced-AI products for enterprises and governments today. It is […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Global control via quantum actuators
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article introduces an elegant solution to one of quantum computing's biggest architectural challenges: how to perform selective quantum operations without needing individual control over every qubit. The proposed quantum actuators act like smart intermediaries that can temporarily store and release energy on command, allowing processors to activate specific multi-qubit gates even when using simple global control fields that affect all qubits simultaneously. This approach could dramatically simplify the control electronics needed for large-scale quantum computers while actually enhancing their connectivity and enabling long-range quantum operations between distant qubits. The connection to quantum thermodynamics through the "quantum battery" interpretation adds theoretical depth and suggests these actuators might serve dual purposes in future quantum processors.

We introduce the concept of quantum actuators as mediators for globally controlled quantum computation. Auxiliary quantum systems act as controllable elements that transiently store and release interaction energy, enabling the selective activation of multi-qubit gates within globally driven architectures. During compilation they remain passive and require no fine-grained local control, while during operation they allow for controlled activation of interactions and directional flow of quantum information. We provide a framework for embedding quantum actuators in globally controlled processors, showing how they enhance connectivity, enable long-range entangling operations, and bridge distant regions without increasing local control overhead. We discuss physical implementations and architectural strategies illustrating how these elements extend the capabilities of global-control schemes. A complementary interpretation in terms of quantum batteries naturally emerges, connecting global-control architectures with concepts from quantum thermodynamics while highlighting the distinct operational role of quantum actuators.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: UK Lawmaker Proposes U.K.-U.S. Quantum Partnership Can be a Strategic Counterweight to China
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights how quantum computing is increasingly viewed through the lens of national security and international competition, with policymakers recognizing that quantum supremacy could reshape global power dynamics. Tugendhat's proposal for deeper U.K.-U.S. quantum collaboration reflects growing concerns about China's substantial investments in quantum research and the strategic importance of maintaining Western leadership in this critical technology. The mention of Quantinuum as a model partnership underscores how the quantum industry is already operating across borders, suggesting that formal government partnerships could amplify existing commercial and research ties. This signals a shift from viewing quantum computing as purely a scientific endeavor to recognizing it as a cornerstone of future economic and military competitiveness.

Insider Brief A transatlantic partnership between the U.S. and the U.K. could serve as a fulcrum in the global effort to develop quantum computing, with Quantinuum positioned as a leading example of how combined capabilities may accelerate progress and shape geopolitical competition. In a Wall Street Journal opinion column, Tom Tugendhat, a member of the […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Study Finds More Qubits Yield Better Financial Optimization Outcomes
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article represents a crucial milestone in quantum computing's journey from laboratory curiosity to commercial viability, demonstrating that more qubits directly translate to better real-world performance in financial optimization problems. The collaboration between IonQ and Kipu Quantum shows quantum systems moving beyond proof-of-concept demonstrations to deliver measurable improvements over classical methods in portfolio optimization, one of the most promising near-term quantum applications. What makes this particularly significant is the clear scaling behavior observed when expanding from 36 to 64 qubits, suggesting that as quantum hardware continues to improve, financial institutions may soon see compelling reasons to adopt quantum solutions. This direct comparison with classical methods provides the kind of concrete evidence that enterprise decision-makers need to justify quantum computing investments.

Insider Brief Quantum hardware is beginning to show measurable gains on real financial problems, with larger qubit systems delivering better portfolio outcomes in direct comparisons with classical methods. A blog post, detailing results from a collaboration involving IonQ and Kipu Quantum, reports that increasing the size of executable quantum subproblems — from 36 to 64 […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: What Quantum Technology Actually is and Why it Matters
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article addresses a critical communication challenge facing the quantum industry: the tendency to oversimplify "quantum technology" as synonymous with quantum computing alone. While quantum computers capture headlines, quantum sensing and quantum communications are already delivering real-world applications in everything from medical imaging to secure financial transactions, yet they often get overlooked in mainstream discussions. As governments worldwide develop quantum strategies and allocate billions in funding, having precise terminology becomes essential for effective policy-making and public understanding. The piece serves as a valuable primer for distinguishing between quantum computing's future promises and the quantum technologies already transforming industries today.

Insider Brief The term “quantum technology” has entered mainstream discourse with increasing frequency, appearing in government policy documents, corporate strategy announcements, venture capital portfolios, and media headlines. However, the phrase often gets used imprecisely, sometimes referring exclusively to quantum computing while ignoring equally transformative developments in sensing and communications. Quantum technology is not a single […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Bell Experiments Revisited: A Numerical Approach Based on De Broglie--Bohm Theory
item.description:
Curator's Take: This pedagogical study offers a fascinating numerical demonstration of how the de Broglie-Bohm interpretation of quantum mechanics can reproduce the famous Bell inequality violations that seem to rule out local hidden variable theories. By providing explicit simulations of particle trajectories and spin dynamics within this deterministic framework, the researchers bridge a crucial gap between abstract quantum theory and concrete visualization of entanglement phenomena. While de Broglie-Bohm theory remains a minority interpretation among physicists due to its requirement of nonlocal interactions, this work serves as an excellent educational tool for understanding how different interpretations can yield identical experimental predictions while maintaining fundamentally different pictures of physical reality. The numerical approach makes these subtle conceptual distinctions more accessible to students and researchers grappling with the foundational puzzles of quantum mechanics.

We present a complete and rigorous model of an EPR--Bell-type experiment within the framework of the de Broglie--Bohm theory. The purpose of this work is to show explicitly how a deterministic hidden-variable theory can reproduce all quantum-mechanical predictions, including the violation of Bell inequalities. Combining analytical arguments with numerical simulations, our approach offers a unified and transparent illustration of the central ingredients of de Broglie--Bohm theory, including particle trajectories, spin dynamics, and quantum entanglement. The model is designed to be pedagogical and self-contained, making it suitable for readers seeking a concrete understanding of how a nonlocal hidden-variable theory can describe the EPR--Bell experiment and illustrate Bell's theorem.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: 11 Photonic Quantum Computing Companies to Know [2026]
item.description:
Curator's Take: This comprehensive overview arrives at a pivotal moment for photonic quantum computing, as the field matures beyond proof-of-concept demonstrations toward practical systems that could sidestep many challenges facing superconducting and trapped-ion approaches. The diversity of strategies highlighted—from PsiQuantum's silicon photonic architecture to Xanadu's squeezed light approach—underscores how the photonic quantum landscape remains wide open, with no clear dominant paradigm yet emerging. What makes photonic systems particularly compelling is their potential for room-temperature operation and natural integration with existing fiber optic infrastructure, positioning them as strong candidates for distributed quantum networks and cloud-based quantum computing. The article's focus on 11 distinct companies also illustrates the significant commercial momentum building behind photonic quantum technologies, even as fundamental challenges like photon loss and deterministic single-photon generation continue to drive intense research and development efforts.

Insider Brief Photonic quantum computing uses photons instead of matter-based qubits, offering room-temperature operation, fiber-network compatibility, and multiple architectural approaches under active development. A global group of companies including PsiQuantum, Xanadu, and Quandela are advancing distinct photonic strategies spanning silicon photonics, squeezed light, and single-photon systems. Key technical barriers remain around photon loss, deterministic generation, […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Dynamical Evolution of Quantum Correlations and Decoherence in Coupled Oscillators Interacting with a Thermal Reservoir
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research provides crucial insights into how quantum information degrades in realistic noisy environments, which is fundamental to understanding the practical limits of quantum technologies. The finding that quantum discord proves more resilient than entanglement under decoherence is particularly significant, as it suggests that certain quantum advantages might persist even when the more fragile entangled states are lost to environmental noise. The systematic study of how different parameters like temperature, coupling strength, and initial squeezing affect quantum correlation survival offers valuable guidance for designing quantum systems that can maintain their quantum properties longer in the real world. These results directly inform efforts to build more robust quantum computers, sensors, and communication systems by revealing which quantum resources are most likely to survive the inevitable battle against decoherence.

We investigate the dynamical evolution of quantum discord, entanglement and purity in an open quantum system of two coupled asymmetric harmonic oscillators interacting with a thermal environment. Using the Kossakowski-Lindblad master equation we analyze the time evolution starting with a squeezed vacuum state. In contrast to our previous study on entanglement evolution in asymmetric oscillators, the present work introduces XY-type position-position coupling together with a systematic joint analysis of quantum discord and purity alongside entanglement. We examine the combined effects of the squeezing parameter, asymmetry parameter, coupling constant, dissipation rate and temperature. We find that quantum discord and entanglement exhibit, in general, a non-monotonic decrease over time. Increasing temperature consistently accelerates the degradation of both quantum correlations and purity, whereas increasing dissipation accelerates the degradation of quantum correlations but leads to higher steady-state purity. Increasing the squeezing parameter provides a protective effect by enhancing initial correlations and prolonging entanglement survival time, while increasing the coupling constant leads to higher quantum correlations. The asymmetry parameter exhibits only a weak influence on the correlation evolution. Our analysis reveals that quantum discord demonstrates stronger resilience than entanglement, which can present more complex behaviour including entanglement sudden death and possible temporary revivals and re-suppressions. These findings provide valuable insights for developing robust quantum information protocols and strategies for preserving quantum correlations in realistic open quantum systems, with potential extensions to non-Markovian regimes and multi-mode architectures.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: STAR-Magic Mutation: Even More Efficient Analog Rotation Gates for Early Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computer
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most pressing challenges in early fault-tolerant quantum computing: efficiently implementing rotation gates, which are essential for many quantum algorithms but notoriously expensive in error-corrected systems. The STAR-magic mutation protocol achieves a remarkable improvement in error scaling, reducing execution time and error rates by two orders of magnitude for small-angle rotations compared to standard approaches using magic state distillation. This breakthrough is particularly significant for quantum simulation algorithms that rely on Trotter decomposition, where sequences of many small rotation gates are required, potentially making previously impractical simulations feasible on near-term fault-tolerant devices. The work also introduces a novel quantum architecture that cleverly combines different gate synthesis approaches, suggesting we may be closer to practical quantum advantage in simulation tasks than previously thought.

We introduce STAR-magic mutation, an efficient protocol for implementing logical rotation gates on early fault-tolerant quantum computers. This protocol judiciously combines two of the latest state preparation protocols: transversal multi-rotation protocol and magic state cultivation. It achieves a logical rotation gate with a favorable error scaling of $\mathcal{O}(θ_L^{2(1-Θ(1/d))}p_{\text{ph}})$, while requiring only the ancillary space of a single surface code patch. Here, $θ_L$ is the logical rotation angle, $p_{\text{ph}}$ is the physical error rate, and $d$ is the code distance. This scaling marks a significant improvement over the previous state-of-the-art, $\mathcal{O}(θ_L p_{\text{ph}})$, making our protocol particularly powerful for implementing a sequence of small-angle rotation gates, like Trotter-based circuits. Notably, for $θ_L \lesssim 10^{-5}$, our protocol achieves a two-order-of-magnitude reduction in both the execution time and the error rate of analog rotation gates compared to the standard $T$-gate synthesis using cultivated magic states. Building upon this protocol, we also propose a novel quantum computing architecture designed for early fault-tolerant quantum computers, dubbed ``STAR ver.~3". It employs a refined circuit compilation strategy based on Clifford+$T$+$φ$ gate set, rather than the conventional Clifford+$T$ or Clifford+$φ$ gate sets. We establish a theoretical bound on the feasible circuit size on this architecture and illustrate its capabilities by analyzing the spacetime costs for simulating the dynamics of quantum many-body systems. Specifically, we demonstrate that our architecture can simulate biologically-relevant molecules or lattice models at scales beyond the reach of exact classical simulation, with only a few hundred thousand physical qubits, even assuming a realistic error rate of $p_{\text{ph}}=10^{-3}$.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Encoded Quantum Signal Processing for Heisenberg-Limited Metrology
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a breakthrough approach to quantum sensing that could finally make ultra-precise quantum measurements practical in noisy, real-world environments. The researchers cleverly combine quantum error correction with signal processing, showing how to maintain the theoretical Heisenberg limit for measurement precision even when quantum sensors are corrupted by noise - a persistent challenge that has limited the practical deployment of quantum-enhanced metrology. Their "encoded quantum signal processing" framework essentially treats noisy quantum sensors like a single, robust logical sensor, using error syndromes not just to detect problems but as the actual measurement signal. This work could unlock quantum-enhanced sensing applications in everything from gravitational wave detection to medical imaging, where maintaining quantum advantages despite environmental noise has been the key roadblock.

Entangled quantum probes can achieve Heisenberg-limited measurement precision, but this advantage is typically destroyed by noise.
